coredata.dc:title: Understanding the growth of carbon dots derived from bioresources via plasma-electrified synthesis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Akmal, Muhammad Hussnain
Kurniawan, Darwin
Sharma, Neha
Chiang, Wei-Hung
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have been the forefront of materials research owing to their unique size-dependent quantum confinement and carbon hybridization state-dependent physicochemical properties, rendering them useful for many applications, including imaging, sensing, energy conversion and storage, optoelectronics, and nanocatalysis. However, precise atomic-scale control of CDs with well-defined structures and properties is still challenging owing to inefficient synthesis methods and limited understanding of their growth mechanisms. Here, we utilize a direct current (DC) microplasma electrochemical reactor to convert various bioresources into CDs with controlled structures and carbon hybridization states in a rapid, catalyst-free, and environmentally friendly manner. Moreover, in situ optical emission and absorption spectroscopies were further integrated into the microplasma reactor to reveal growth mechanisms, providing a possible prediction over the synthesized products without time-consuming ex situ characterization. A high plasma current enhanced the ionization rate, which subsequently led to more water dissociation into OH radicals, more precursor fragmentation, and therefore higher CDs production. Our work provides insight into the synthesis of bioresource-derived CDs between the plasma parameters and structural properties of the synthesized CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microplasmas
Nanocarbons
Carbon phases
Bioresources

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and ex situ characterization of CDs
In situ studies on the synthesis of bioresource-derived CDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: M.H.A. and D.K. contributed equally to this work. This work was supported by National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan (NSTC; grant nos. NSTC 111-2223-E-011-002-MY3, NSTC 111-2628-E-011-002-MY2, NSTC 109-2923-E-011-003-MY3, NSTC 111-2811-E-011-018, NSTC 111-NU-E-011-001-NU) and National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (NTUST). We also acknowledge Ms. Ju-Hung Ho who operated XPS (ULVAC-PHI. Inc. / PHI 5000 VersaProbe III) at the Precious Instrumentation Center of NTUST.

M.H.A. and D.K. contributed equally to this work. This work was supported by National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan (NSTC; grant nos. NSTC 111-2223-E-011-002-MY3, NSTC 112-2811-E-011-018-MY2, NSTC 113-2927-I-011-504, NSTC 114-NU-E-011-001-NU) and National Taiwan University of Science and Technology (NTUST). We also acknowledge Ms. Ju-Hung Ho who operated XPS (ULVAC-PHI. Inc./PHI 5000 VersaProbe III) at the Precious Instrumentation Center of NTUST, as well as Ms. Chia-Ying Chien of NSTC for the assistance in FE-TEM experiment in National Taiwan University (NTU).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of plasma synthesis of bioresource-derived CDs complemented with detailed in situ diagnostics and ex situ characterization.

Ex situ optical characterization of bioresource-derived CDs. (a) Representative absorption spectra of five different bioresource-derived CDs synthesized at 9.5 mA for 60 min. Representative PL maps of CDs synthesized using (b) glucose, (c) fructose, (d) sucrose, (e) maltose, and (f) citric acid.

Synthesis study of bioresource-derived CDs. Production amount of bioresource-derived CDs as a function of (a) discharge current at a fixed reaction time of 60 min, (b) reaction time at a fixed discharge current of 9.5 mA, (c) NaCl, (d) HCl, and (e) NaOH concentration at a fixed precursor concentration of 67 mM, as well as (f) precursor concentration at a fixed NaOH concentration of 40 mM. (a–b) show the plasma physical parameters, while (c–f) show the plasma chemical parameters.
Ex situ microRaman measurements of bioresource-derived CDs. Raman spectra of bioresource-derived CDs synthesized at (a) 5, (b) 8, and (c) 9.5 mA for 60 min.

TEM study of four selected bioresource-derived CDs. TEM images of (a) glucose-derived CDs synthesized at 9.5 mA, (b) fructose-derived CDs synthesized at 8 mA, (c) sucrose-derived CDs synthesized at 9.5 mA, and (d) maltose-derived CDs synthesized at 9.5 mA for 60 min. The bottom left and right images in (a–d) show the corresponding FFT and filtered inverse FFT images, respectively. The scale bars represent 2 nm.

Functionality study of bioresource-derived carbon dots (CDs). XPS measurements of bioresource-derived CDs with (a) Full-survey scan and (b) high-resolution XPS measurement of C1s of bioresource-derived CDs. (c) FTIR spectra of bioresource-derived CDs.

Time-resolved in situ diagnostics for synthesis of bioresource-derived CDs using different plasma currents. (a) Integrated OES spectra during plasma treatment of maltose for 60 min at different currents. (b) Time series of integrated plasma intensity from 200 to 1000 nm and (c) integrated OH intensity taken from 304 to 307 nm during plasma treatment of maltose for 60 min at different currents. (d) In-situ absorption spectra of maltose-derived CDs at 0–3.5 min of reaction at 9.5 mA discharge current. (e) Time series of the integrated absorbance from 330 to 400 nm during plasma treatment of maltose at different currents.

Correlations between the OH radical intensities and properties of bioresource-derived CDs. Effect of plasma OH intensities on (a) absorbance, (b) ID/IG, (c) PL emission wavelengths, and (d) PLQY of bioresource-derived CDs. The data presented are summarized for various plasma currents (5, 8, and 9.5 mA) and reaction times (15, 30, 45, and 60 min).

head.ce:title: Understanding the growth of carbon dots derived from bioresources via plasma-electrified synthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607 Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sustainable Electrochemical Energy Development (SEED) Center, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sustainable Electrochemical Energy Development (SEED) Center, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei City 10607, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemical Engineering, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, Taipei, 10607, Taiwan.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Equal contribution. There two authors contributed to this article equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been the forefront of materials research owing to their unique size-dependent quantum confinement and carbon hybridization state-dependent physicochemical properties, rendering them useful for many applications, including imaging, sensing, energy conversion and storage, optoelectronics, and nanocatalysis. However, precise atomic-scale control of CDs with well-defined structures and properties is still challenging owing to inefficient synthesis methods and limited understanding of their growth mechanisms. Here, we utilize a direct current (DC) microplasma electrochemical reactor to convert various bioresources into CDs with controlled structures and carbon hybridization states in a rapid, catalyst-free, and environmentally friendly manner. Moreover, in situ optical emission and absorption spectroscopies were further integrated into the microplasma reactor to reveal growth mechanisms, providing a possible prediction over the synthesized products without time-consuming ex situ characterization. A high plasma current enhanced the ionization rate, which subsequently led to more water dissociation into OH radicals, more precursor fragmentation, and therefore higher CDs production. Our work provides insight into the synthesis of bioresource-derived CDs between the plasma parameters and structural properties of the synthesized CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new generation of carbon nanomaterials that have attracted much scientific and engineering interest owing to their unique size-dependent quantum confinement [1,2], as well as hybridization state-dependent (sp2 and/or sp3) spin-orbit coupling and electron-electron correlation [3,4]. Consequently, CDs with different sp2/sp3 ratios can exhibit distinctive optical, electronic, and magnetic properties required for specific applications, including sensing [5,6], electrochemical energy storage [7], catalysis, and electronic devices [4]. The size dependent emission properties of CDs further enhance their application horizons [8].Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and nanodiamonds (NDs) are two types of CDs hybridized mainly in sp2 and sp3 carbon configurations, respectively. Coupled with ease of functionalization and excellent biocompatibility, both GQDs and NDs not only have been widely applied for the aforementioned applications, but also for biomedical applications such as imaging, therapy, drug delivery, and antioxidation [4,9,10]. These diamond materials have also shown temperature-dependent luminescence properties for optical storage applications [11].

The two main synthesis approaches for CDs include top-down and bottom-up approaches. The top-down approach involves abrasive disintegration and exfoliation of readily available, high-cost, and nonrenewable bulk carbon, including carbon fiber [12] and graphite [13]. Despite ensuring mass production, a top-down approach is still limited by the utilization of strong oxidizers, concentrated acids, high temperatures, tedious processes, and difficulty in controlling the morphology and size [14]. The bottom-up approach utilizes small hydrocarbons or polyaromatic hydrocarbons as precursors to synthesize CDs through carbonization, pyrolysis, and stepwise chemical reactions [15,16]. Although this approach enables control over the size, edge, and surface functionalities, the use of high temperatures, long reaction times, toxic chemicals, and complicated procedures hamper its practicality for synthesizing CDs. Moreover, the currently available synthesis methods still face an elusive understanding of the fundamental growth of CDs, causing precise atomic-scale control of CDs with well-defined structures and properties that are challenging.

The critical requirement for the sustainable synthesis of nanomaterials has contributed to the interest in bioresources derived carbon dots (CDs). However, accurate control on structures remained elusive with the conventional methods. The plasma-electrified approach employs earth-abundant precursor and a catalyst-free process that offers scalability and environmental friendliness while solving resource and performance issues in carbon nanotechnology. Microplasmas are a unique class of gaseous discharge with one of its dimensions less than a millimeter scale and can generate a large amount of highly reactive species (i.e., electrons, ions, and radicals) under ambient conditions [17], rendering it useful for the synthesis of various nanomaterials including metal nanoparticles, silicon QDs, carbon dots, GQDs, and NDs without any additional high-cost catalysts, toxic chemicals, high temperatures and pressures, and long reaction times [18–23]. Coupled with the use of earth-abundant bioresources as carbon precursors, microplasmas can be effective for the sustainable production of CDs [17]. Moreover, microplasmas can also be scaled up by advancing towards a plasma array consisting of tens to hundreds of plasma jets and/or integrating with microfluidic devices [24,25]. In line with the challenges of current carbon dots synthesis, our plasma-electrification approach employs microplasma to convert the bio-resources into CDs in just a few minutes under ambient conditions. It maintains no catalysts and is therefore sustainable which allows great precision in sp2/sp3 tuneability. The method has advantages over conventional methods in terms of simplicity and environmental impact. It comes from green nanotechnology and gives a large-scale process with controllable structures.

Fructose (≥99 %) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid (99.5 %), and maltose (≥92 %) were purchased from Thermo Scientific. Sucrose (≥99 %), and glucose (≥99 %) were purchased from Acros Organics. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥98 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5 %), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were obtained from Honeywell Fluka. Pt foil (20 × 20 × 0.1 mm) was obtained from Guv Team International.

Mother electrolyte solutions including acidic, basic, and neutral (i.e. hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and sodium chloride (NaCl), respectively) were prepared with deionized (DI) water at a fixed concentration of 1 M. During the experiments, various concentrations (i.e., 20, 40, 80, 100 mM) of these standard solutions were employed to study their effect on the synthesis process. Meanwhile, bioresources (fructose, glucose, sucrose, maltose and citric acid) were used as the carbon precursor. Their mother solutions were also prepared by mixing the respective precursor with DI water at a fixed concentration of 1 M. For the synthesis, various concentrations including 35, 67, 100, and 200 mM were employed by diluting the mother solution with DI water and different electrolytes with certain concentrations. All chemicals were used as received without any purifications. One fresh solution was prepared just prior to each experiment to maintain consistency in all measurements.

Phase selective CDs were synthesized using a customized direct current (dc) Ar microplasma electrochemical reactor with a gas flow rate of 25 standard cubic centimetres per minute (sccm) (Fig. 1). A hollow capillary tube with an inner diameter of 178 μm and a Pt foil were used as the cathode and anode, respectively. Around 1/3 of the Pt foil was immersed in an electrolyte solution to complete the electrochemical circuit. The plasma was ignited on the electrolyte surface and maintained by a high-voltage DC power supply connected to a high ballast resistor of 160 kΩ in the current limiting mode. To study the effect of the plasma physical parameters, the reaction was conducted by varying the discharge currents (5, 6.5, 8, and 9.5 mA) and reaction times (15, 30, 45, and 60 min), while fixing the precursor and electrolyte concentrations at 67 and 40 mM, respectively. On the other hand, the plasma chemical parameters were investigated at different precursor (35, 67, 100, and 200 mM) and electrolyte (20, 40, 80, and 100 mM) concentrations. After plasma treatment, the solution was neutralized and dialyzed against DI water for 2 days. The outer solution was replaced with fresh deionized (DI) water every 6 h. All the syntheses were conducted for three times.

The gas-phase in situ measurements were performed using a time-resolved OES technique by collecting the plasma emission via a collimating lens connected to a commercial spectrometer (Ocean Optics HR4000 spectrometer) (Fig. 1). The measurements were conducted throughout the reaction, with an integration time of 100 ms and 100 accumulations. The liquid-phase in situ measurements were performed via UV–Vis absorption using a commercial spectrometer (Ocean Optics HR4000 spectrometer) coupled with a UV–Vis light source (Ocean Optics DT-MINI-2-GS, Deuterium Tungsten Halogen light source). The measurements were focused at a certain depth below the liquid interface at an integration time of 50 ms and 20 accumulations. All the data shown are based on the averaged results from three times measurement with the corresponding standard deviations are presented as error bars.

Optical characterization of the colloidal bioresource-derived CDs was carried out using photoluminescence (PL) mapping via a commercial spectrofluorometer with a photomultiplier tube (PMT) detector (Nanolog-3, HORIBA Scientific, Japan). The absorbance spectra of all samples were collected using a JASCO V676 absorbance spectrophotometer and a matched set of 1 cm path-length quartz cuvettes. MicroRaman measurements were performed at room temperature using a JASCO 5100 spectrometer at a laser excitation wavelength of 532 nm. All the results from the above measurements are based on the averaged results from three times measurement with the corresponding standard deviations are presented as error bars. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using a field-emission gun FEI Tecnai G2 F-20 S-TWIN and JEOL JEM-2100F operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a PHI 5000 Versa Probe III (ULVACPHI, Inc.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and ex situ characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Several earth-abundant bioresources, including glucose, fructose, sucrose, maltose, and citric acid, have been used as precursors for the plasma synthesis of bioresource-derived CDs. Ex situ characterization, including absorption and PL spectroscopy, was performed to evaluate the optical characteristics of the resulting CDs, as shown in Fig. 2 . The results displayed are for the samples synthesized at 9.5 mA for 60 min. Characteristic features from absorption spectroscopy include a broad peak at around 278 nm, representing the π-π∗ electronic transition from conjugated π-electrons in sp2 carbon (Fig. 2a). Meanwhile, the long absorption tail ranging between 300 and 500 nm can be attributed to the n-π∗ transition from oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs, which can affect the electronic and optical properties of the as-synthesized CDs [27]. The PL emission of the synthesized CDs can be visually observed under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S1). PL maps shown in Fig. 2b–f reveal excitation-independent emissions for all bioresource-derived CDs, suggesting a uniform band transition distribution. Interestingly, the emission spectra were influenced by the precursors used. For instance, the emission with the strongest PL intensity for CDs derived from all saccharide groups is located at approximately 440 nm (Fig. 2b–e), suggesting a compositional similarity across these CDs. This PL can be possibly attributed to the hydroxyl groups related to hybridized states [28]. It is worthy to note that the furanose structure of fructose possesses lower C–H bond dissociation enthalpies than the pyranose structure of glucose due to its higher strain destabilization in molecule [29]. Therefore, a more efficient fragment generation, CDs conversion, and better carbon core arrangement can be anticipated when fructose is used as the precursor. Only the CDs derived from citric acid exhibited local PL emission with the strongest intensity at a longer wavelength of approximately 525 nm, which may be due to the structural difference from that of tricarboxylic acid. The presence of carboxylic groups, which are absent in saccharides, helps stabilize the aromatic structure domains through resonance, enhancing the delocalization of π-electrons across the conjugated system and leading to red-shifted PL emission caused by the lower energy transitions [30]. The corresponding PL quantum yield (PLQY) was also estimated to be 0.65, 0.9, 0.48, 0.59, and 0.68 % for glucose-, fructose-, sucrose-, maltose-, and citric acid-derived CDs, respectively, relative to quinine sulfate (Table S1) [31]. The higher PLQY of fructose-derived CDs, can be possible attributed to the abundance hydroxyl and carbonyl groups in fructose-derived CDs coupled with the orderly carbon core arrangement. These functional groups are promising for sensing and diagnostic applications because they can improve the emissive sites and increase the solubility and stability in aqueous environments.

To understand the effect of the plasma parameters on the synthesis of CDs, we first investigated the plasma physical parameters by changing the discharge current but fixing the reaction time, and vice versa. Fig. 3 a shows the production yield of CDs at a fixed reaction time of 60 min, but at discharge currents of 5, 6.5, 8, and 9.5 mA. The results indicate that the increase in current leads to a higher synthesis yield, which can be attributed to the enhanced collisional rate between atoms and electrons at higher discharge currents, resulting in an increased number of reactive species interacting with the solution at elevated kinetic energies. Meanwhile, Fig. 3b suggests an increasing production yield with a longer reaction time, because more energy generated by the plasma can be dissipated into the electrolyte solution. Because the plasma-based electrochemical reactor relies on the presence of active species to initiate the chemical reaction, a greater number of those reactive species will result in a higher decomposition rate of the precursor and thereby a higher production yield. In addition, a higher kinetic energy also ensures that the system can overcome the activation reaction energy barrier, leading to the catalyst-free synthesis of CDs.

After understanding the effect of the plasma physical parameters, the chemical parameters were investigated by changing the electrolyte types and concentrations while fixing the reaction time and discharge current at 60 min and 9.5 mA, respectively. The electrolyte type seems to play a significant role in the synthesis of CDs by plasma. It can be observed that NaCl electrolyte exhibits almost similar production yield of CDs at different NaCl concentrations (Fig. 3c), while HCl electrolyte shows a declining production yield of CDs with increasing concentration of HCl (Fig. 3d). In contrast, the NaOH electrolyte was the most effective in producing CDs by microplasma, and the amount of CDs produced increased proportionally with the NaOH concentration (Fig. 3e). The increase in NaOH concentration can simultaneously lead to an increase in electrical conductivity, generation of OH radicals due to enhanced water dissociation, and better energy transfer from plasma to the electrolyte. Consequently, higher carbon fragment generation and CDs production can be expected [32]. As the concentration of HCl increased, the electrolyte pH became more acidic because of the higher amount of H+ in the solution. The plasma can produce eaq −, which have been reported to initiate carbon-carbon bond formation through pinacol coupling [33]. Therefore, the results shown in Fig. 3d might imply neutralization of eaq − by H+, reducing the amount of CDs produced under acidic conditions [34]. This is likely to also be the case for citric acid, where it exhibits the lowest CDs production amount (Fig. 3c and d) and even none at higher HCl concentrations (Fig. 3e). The strong acidity of citric acid lowers the efficacy of eaq − produced during the plasma treatment by impeding carbon-carbon bond formation that consequently results in fewer CDs formation compared with other bioresources having less acidic characteristics.

Note that although 100 mM NaOH resulted in the highest production of CDs, 40 mM NaOH was chosen to study the effect of precursor concentration to avoid saturation during the absorbance measurement, which can cause inaccurate results and data interpretation. The results presented in Fig. 3f reveal that the production amount of CDs tends to increase with increasing precursor concentration before stagnating at high concentration of 200 mM, except for citric acid, which is almost constant. It is imperative that despite resulting in higher concentration of CDs for maltose (Fig. 3), its production yield seems lower compared with fructose (Fig. S2). This discrepancy is due to the difference in the precursors' molecular weights and structural properties. Maltose possesses larger molecular weight (342.30 g/mol) than fructose (180.16 g/mol), resulting in a greater amount of precursor mass under the same concentration. Since more initial mass is used in the synthesis of maltose-derived CDs, higher amount of CDs produced can be expected, yet it unnecessarily results in higher production yield. The higher production yield in fructose-derived CDs can possibly be attributed to the lower dissociation enthalpies of C–H bonds in furanose than in pyranose [29], enabling more efficient fragments generation and CDs conversion. Based on these results, it is possible to employ larger concentrations of NaOH to permit larger production of CDs. In contrast, increasing the concentration of precursors can result in a surplus of unreacted molecules and possibly reduced electrolyte conductivity, leading to a plateau in product quantity (Fig. 3) and even lower production yield (Fig. S2). Based on the above results, the electrolyte type and concentration can be considered as the main chemical parameters to promote an efficient synthesis of CDs.

Raman spectroscopy was employed to investigate the possibility of controlling the carbon hybridization state of CDs by plasma, and the Raman spectra of the CDs are depicted in Fig. 4 . Two Raman features can be observed, including G- and D-bands, which span from 1575 to 1600 cm−1 and 1350 to 1360 cm−1, respectively. Generally, the D band refers to defects within the graphitic lattice such as vacancies, sp3 carbon phases, and functional groups. On the other hand, the G-band represents the E2g phonon mode or in-plane stretching mode of sp2 carbon within the graphitic lattice [4]. Thus, the intensity ratio between the D- and G-bands (ID/IG) can be used as a quick screening method to evaluate the sp2/sp3 carbon hybridization state in the synthesized CDs. All bioresource-derived CDs seemed to show a relatively low ID/IG with a value of less than one, except for fructose-derived CDs synthesized at 8 mA for 60 min, which possessed the highest ID/IG. These results suggest the possibility of tuning these ID/IG values by simply modulating the synthesis parameters, such as the discharge current and precursor type. The adjustable sp3/sp2 ratio of CDs is beneficial for certain applications. High sp3/sp2 ratio suggests a possible nanodiamond-like structure having enhanced mechanical stability and tunable electronic properties, while low sp3/sp2 ratio suggests a predominant graphene-like structure having improved electrical conductivity.

TEM measurements were performed to understand the morphology and lattice structure of the synthesized CDs. Four samples, including glucose-, fructose-, sucrose-, and maltose-derived CDs, were used as representatives for the TEM measurements. All the samples were synthesized at 9.5 mA for 60 min, except for the fructose-derived CDs, which were synthesized at 8 mA for 60 min owing to their high ID/IG values. The low-magnification TEM images show well dispersed particle-liked nanostructures with narrow size distribution (Fig. S3). The observed clear lattice fringes from many particles indicated the formation of ordered and crystalline nanostructures in the synthesized carbon dots. Our results also revealed the presence of both graphitic and diamond phases, as indicated by HRTEM images and Raman analysis. Overall, the TEM result is in agreed with Raman analysis. This result suggests the possibility of tuning different carbon phases using our plasma-assisted approach. The small particle size within the range of 2.9–3.8 nm generally can exhibit an emission at ∼450 nm (deep blue region), as reflected from PL maps in Fig. 2. This small particle size coupled with the presence of function groups can contribute to a broad D- and G-bands in the Raman spectrum (Fig. 4) caused by the phonon confinement effect [35]. The narrow size distribution shown also suggests size uniformity in the bioresource-derived CDs. Moreover, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images in Fig. 5 reveal a clear lattice spacing in the range of 0.21–0.22 nm, which can be attributed to the (111) plane of cubic diamond, as well as (100) and (002) planes of lonsdaleite [36]. However, those lattice spacing, at the same time, can also be assigned to the (100) diffraction pattern of graphene [37]. On the other hand, the lattice spacing of 0.24 nm observed can be ascribed to the (11
2 ‾
0) hexagonal lattice of graphene, while the slightly larger lattice spacing of 0.27 nm can be reasoned due to the presence of functional groups and defects [38]. Similarly, this 0.27 nm lattice spacing is also consistent with the (200) plane of face-centered cubic (FCC) diamonds [39]. Consequently, it is challenging to differentiate graphitic structures from diamond structures based only on TEM results. Nonetheless, a combination of Raman and TEM analyses suggested that the fructose-derived CDs might consist of a mixture of both nanographene and ND structures (Figs. 4b and 5b).

XPS measurements were performed on all bioresource-derived CDs to investigate their chemical bond configurations. Fig. 6 a displays the full-survey scan of the five bioresource-derived CDs, showing a C1s peak at approximately 284 eV and an O1s peak at approximately 530 eV. The observed Na peak was due to the leftover Na from the NaOH electrolyte used during the synthesis, which was bonded to the oxygen-containing functional groups of the CDs. Meanwhile, the presence of a minimized N peak at approximately 400 nm can be attributed to the adsorbed nitrogen species. It is possible that this nitrogen was deposited on the CD surface during the synthesis, because of the open-air reaction where the plasma can have a direct contact with the surrounding air, leading to electron-impacted ionizations of nitrogen, as evidenced by the high emission intensity of nitrogen 2nd positive system in Fig. 7 a and Figure S4. Since this nitrogen is only adsorbed to the CD surface, the PL properties of the corresponding CDs will not be affected.

High-resolution XPS measurements were performed on the C1s peak of the bioresource-derived CDs (Fig. 6b). It is apparent that fructose-, sucrose-, and maltose-derived CDs possess a considerable amount of sp3 hybridized carbon, which can probably be attributed to the presence of a diamond structure, as reflected by the observation of the (111) plane of cubic diamond in the TEM image (Fig. 5b–d). In the case of glucose-derived CDs, the XPS spectrum shows a relatively low intensity of sp3 carbon, suggesting that despite the possibility of having an FCC diamond structure (Fig. 5a), the structure is mainly dominated by sp2 carbon. It is plausible that sp2 and sp3 carbon phases can coexist in a single material, highlighting the possibility of controlling the carbon hybridization state through microplasma engineering. In addition, the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs can alter their electronic band structure and surface chemistry [40]. For example, hydroxyl groups favor hydrogen bonding interactions and increase the hydrophilicity of the surface, resulting in altered dispersibility and colloidal stability in aqueous environments [41]. Moreover, CDs may possess acidic properties because of their carboxyl groups, which may customize their chemical reactivity.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were performed to support the XPS results (Fig. 6c). A prominent broad peak between ∼3200 to 3630 cm−1 is typically assigned to O–H stretching vibrations, while a weak peak in the range of ∼2850–2980 cm−1 is attributed to aliphatic C–H stretching [42]. Furthermore, the peak around ∼1070 cm−1 and ∼1730 cm−1 correspond to C–O and CO stretching vibrations, confirming the presence of C–O–C and –COOH in the synthesized CDs, respectively [43,44]. These results correlate well with the XPS analysis (Fig. 6a and b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ studies on the synthesis of bioresource-derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 7a shows the overall integrated plasma emission spectra of the fructose plasma-treated for 60 min under different currents. Several reactive species can be observed including: nitric oxide (NO) bands from 200 to 300 nm; hydroxyl (OH) 1st order rotational band (
A 2
Σ →
X 2
Π
) from 306 to 309 nm; nitrogen (N2) (
C 3
Π u
→
B 3
Π g
) band, also known as the N2 2nd positive system, from 311 to 470 nm; N2 (
B 3
Π g
→
A 3
Σ u +
) band from 625 to 688 nm; the hydrogen atom Balmer line (Hα) around 656 nm; several Ar I lines (
4 p → 4 s
) from 690 to 860 nm; and atomic oxygen (O) peak around 777 nm. The presence of these species in the plasma column was due to collisional interactions between energetic electrons and ionized argon with the surrounding air molecules. These metastable reactive species provide a possible pathway for plasma-assisted chemical decomposition of the precursor by lodging their energy to the impacting precursor structure, starting from precursor fragmentation to the nucleation and growth of CDs in a catalyst-free manner. Active chemical radicals can also be generated via electrically excited molecular reactions [45]. The negligible Ar I (400–470 nm) implies a low electron temperature in the plasma for local thermal equilibrium [46].

Because the acquisition rate of the OES measurement was every 10 s, the OES spectra were plotted as a function of time to investigate the evolution of the plasma intensity, which might reflect the plasma energy during the synthesis process. The maltose sample was selected as a representative due to its high production yield among other bioresources (Fig. 3). The study was also complemented with a control using blank 40 mM NaOH without any maltose treated by the same 9.5 mA fixed discharge current. The plasma emission becomes stronger with increasing current, which is consistent with a higher ionization rate (Fig. 7b), thus enabling greater energy transmission into the liquid. Despite showing a similar plasma intensity increment over time, there was a reduction in the plasma intensity during the plasma treatment of maltose compared to the blank solution. Because there is no CDs formation in the blank solution, the results shown in Fig. 7b reveal that CDs can only be formed via the unique plasma-liquid chemistry, in which a higher current promotes precursor dissociation at the expense of a lower plasma intensity. This phenomenon can be attributed to the utilization of a portion of the plasma energy in both the dissociation and synthesis processes of the CDs. The elaborate interaction between the plasma energy distribution and formation of new materials emphasizes the dynamic nature of plasma-based processes, underscoring the importance of comprehending and controlling these parameters for precise atomic-scale control over the synthesis of CDs. Because the ionization rate varies with the change in the current, the intensity of the active species responsible for the chemical reactions at the plasma-liquid interface also changes, which can affect the synthesis process. An increase in current leads to an increase in the average kinetic energy of electrons with which they collide with other species, resulting in an increase in plasma intensity.

For instance, the presence of OH radicals in the spectra can be explained by the following equation:
(1) H2O + e− → •OH + H+
where e− is the impacting electron. As the current increased, the electron density also increased, giving rise to an increased collision rate with the water molecules at higher kinetic energies. This, in turn, increases the possibility of H2O electron-impact dissociation, resulting in the formation of additional OH radicals (Fig. S4). A previous study suggested that the OH radical is highly reactive in cleaving the glycosidic linkages of chitosan [47]. Therefore, higher fragmentation of the bioresource precursor can be expected to occur at a higher current owing to more OH radical generation. Note that because the synthesis was conducted under ambient conditions, there is a great possibility that the aforementioned energetic electrons can also interact with the surrounding N2 molecules, enhancing their population in the upper excited state. An increase in the current leads to an increase in the gas temperature owing to the high average electron kinetic energy, making electron-impact ionization more frequent and enhancing energy transfer [48]. It is important to mention that the upper excited state
C 3
Π u
has less lifetime of around 39.3 ns and a high quenching rate makes it highly unstable [49]. Therefore, these two species (OH and N2) are favorable for plasma-assisted chemical reactions. Fig. 7c and Fig. S5 depicts the time evolution of the OH radical and N2 (2nd positive system) integrated over 304–307 nm and 330–338 nm, respectively. These species followed the same trend as the total plasma intensity (Fig. 7b), in which there was an intensity reduction for the plasma-treated fructose compared with the blank, suggesting a possible involvement of these species in the plasma-assisted precursor dissociation and formation of CDs.

It is important to note that OH radicals are highly reactive species possessing a high oxidation ability that can readily cut down precursors into small fragments [50]. The increased OH radical intensity indicates enhanced oxidation of C–C bonds, leading to the creation of C–OH bonds, which can subsequently participate in the formation of C–O–C groups [51] and help in the fragmentation of the precursor. OH radicals also tend to effectively teardown graphene oxide into GQDs [52]. As shown in Fig. 7c, the OH intensity could be regulated by adjusting the plasma time and current. A higher degree of precursor fragmentation can be expected at a higher energy input, which leads to more CDs generation. Additionally, the plasma-assisted excitation of vibrational modes in nitrogen N2 2nd positive system effectively diminishes the dissociation barrier, thereby augmenting the catalytic activity of the plasma [53]. However, it is important to note that the plasma properties are strongly affected by the electrolyte medium. For instance, in situ OES studies of plasma ignited on acidic, neutral, and alkaline media may not be comparable because they may result in different plasma properties (e.g., electron density, electron temperature, and energy distribution). Thus, the correlations between the discharge current and time and the plasma intensity, OH intensity, and N2 intensity were only investigated using five different bioresources at the same concentration (67 mM) dissolved in 40 mM NaOH solution.

In situ absorption measurements were performed to validate this hypothesis. Fig. 7d shows the in situ absorption spectra of the fructose-derived CDs after 7 min of plasma treatment. The data were taken for up to 7 min as they became saturated owing to the limitation of the spectrometer. Initially, no absorption was observed, indicating that no reaction had occurred at this point. Over time, the growth rate increases, indicating that more plasma-assisted reactions occur in the solution, giving rise to the formation of CDs or molecular fragments that become the building blocks of CDs. To evaluate the time evolution of the absorption, the absorption bands from 270 to 400 nm corresponding to π-π∗ and n-π∗ transitions of CDs were further integrated and the results are shown in Fig. 7e. The results reveal a higher absorption at a higher discharge current, validating the above hypothesis that a higher electrical charge can induce more OH radical generation, which subsequently leads to more precursor fragmentation and CDs production. Photoluminescence (PL) tuning of carbon dots (CDs) using plasma parameters has been achieved by producing electronic transitions (Fig. 8 d). OH radicals (Fig. 7c) break accessible precursors into sp2 regions and pi oxygen groups (Fig. 6c), leading to the formation of the transitions π-π∗ (Fig. 2a) and n-π∗ (300–500 nm). Fructose-originated CDs presenting two phases, sp2 and sp3 (Fig. 5b) present higher PLQY (0.9 %) due to the high sp2 formation; citric-acid-conjugated moved up emission to red (more than 525 nm Fig. 2f). More OH reduces defects (Fig. 4) increasing the PLQY. Our data suggest a plasma-mediated CD formation model: (1) increased current elevates OH radical production (Fig. 7c), fragmenting bioresources into carbon intermediates, as absorption rises (Fig. 7e); (2) solvated electrons (eaq −) in NaOH-rich conditions (Fig. 3e) catalyze nucleation via carbon-carbon bonding; (3) plasma conditions, modulated by current and electrolyte, influence sp2 (graphitic) or sp3 (diamond) phase prevalence, as depicted by Raman analysis (Fig. 4) and TEM (Fig. 5). This links plasma energy and chemistry to CD structure with predictive control. Precursor structure drives plasma CD synthesis; lower C–H bond energy of fructose leads to enhanced fragmentation by OH radicals allowing higher production yields (Fig. S2) compared to glucose. Disaccharides (sucrose, maltose) show kinetics retardation due glycosidic cleavage, while the carboxyls of citric acid reduce efficiency by limiting eaq − mediated bonding dissociation (Fig. 3). The resulting pathways will affect the properties of CDs (Fig. 8d).

Based on the above results, we conducted thorough analyses by combining both in situ diagnostic and ex situ characterization to reveal the relationship between the plasma OH intensities and the absorbance, ID/IG, and PL emission wavelengths (Fig. 8). Maltose-derived CDs appear to exhibit the highest absorption with the lowest value of integrated OH intensities, which is the opposite of citric acid-derived CDs (Fig. 8a), even though all syntheses were carried out under the same experimental conditions. This result suggests that plasma conditions might be more suitable for decomposing maltose to produce higher amounts of CDs. Despite this fact, all bioresources seemed to exhibit the same trend, in which the absorbance increased as the OH intensity increased. Interestingly, only fructose-derived CDs possessed the highest ID/IG, as evidenced from Fig. 4b, whereas the other bioresource-derived CDs were clustered within the ID/IG of 0.2–0.8, irrespective of the plasma conditions (Fig. 8b). The higher ID/IG for fructose-derived CDs indicates a higher number of defects present in their structure. However, it is difficult to decouple the nature of the defect in the presence of sp3 carbon, surface functional groups, edges, or vacancies. In the case of PL emission, depicted in Fig. 8c, all the PL emissions from different bioresource-derived CDs did not change much with respect to the OH intensities, which can possibly be attributed to the similar ID/IG. Note that the PL emission of CDs is strongly related to their structure; thus, it is plausible that the similar ID/IG for most cases suggests a similar structure and subsequently similar PL emission wavelengths. A mechanistic diagram (Fig. S6) has been added to elucidate how •OH intensity (from in situ OES, Fig. 7c) affects CD forming: greater •OH intensity gives rise to larger CDs with greater defects (higher ID/IG, Fig. 8b), a red-shift in PL (Fig. 8c), and increased PLQY (Fig. 8d), whereas low •OH intensity yields smaller CDs with fewer defects and a blue shift in PL.

Nonetheless, there is a slight tendency that CDs with higher ID/IG ratio or structural defects at higher OH intensities (Fig. 8b). Interestingly, each bioresource-derived CDs produced at higher OH intensity also tends to exhibit higher PLQY (Fig. 8d). Although the plasma-synthesized CDs prepared by low precursor concentrations exhibited lower PLQY than fluorescent CDs prepared by other methods including microwave-assisted [54], hydrothermal [55], and ultrasonic shearing [56], it is noted that plasma-synthesized CDs prepared with high precursor concentrations could exhibited higher PLQY values. The result suggests that it is possible to tune the PL properties of CDs by controlling the synthesis parameters during plasma synthesis. Based on these results, it is possible that during the growth of CDs, OH radicals also oxidize CDs edges to form oxygen-containing functional groups, which can act as the emissive sites of the CDs. These functional groups suppress the non-radiative recombination centers at the edges of sp2-conjugated π-domains, thus enhancing the overall PLQY [57]. Thus, there is a possibility to improve the PLQY of the resulting CDs by simply adjusting the plasma parameters so that the plasma can generate much higher OH intensity, yet further study is still need to be conducted. Note that the errors presented as error bars in Figs. 3 and 8 can come from the slight differences in precursor electrolyte preparation and sensitivity limitation of the instrumentations. Nevertheless, the relatively small error bars presented suggest the high reproducibility and reliability of our results.

We have established a microplasma engineering to convert various bioresources into CDs in a catalyst-free, environmentally friendly, rapid, and simple manner. The combination of in situ diagnostic (OES and absorbance) with ex situ material characterization (Raman, PL, TEM, and XPS) reveals that the synthesis, chemical phases, and emission properties of plasma-synthesized CDs from different precursors can be affected by active plasma species like OH radicals and N2 (2nd positive system) controlled by plasma conditions including current and time. TEM and Raman studies suggest that the fructose-derived CDs consist of a mixture of graphene and diamond structures, where there is a possibility that the sp3 and sp2 ratio can be tuned by controlling the plasma energy. It is envisaged that these carbon hybridization states can be controlled by modulating the plasma conditions during the synthesis of the CDs. Our work provides insight not only into the selective synthesis of carbon-phase CDs from various bioresources but also an engineering correlation between in situ diagnostic and ex situ characterization.

Muhammad Hussnain Akmal: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Darwin Kurniawan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Methodology, Formal analysis. Neha Sharma: Validation, Investigation. Wei-Hung Chiang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

We also acknowledge Ms. Ju-Hung Ho who operated XPS (ULVAC-PHI.
